sections.heading: Core Concept
sections.content: The core idea of a linked list is: **nodes connected by pointers**.
items: Each node stores a **value** and uses **next** to point to the following node.
Nodes do not need to be contiguous in memory; pointer links define the order.
A singly linked list only moves forward; a doubly linked list adds **prev** so you can move backward.
With a **tail pointer**, appending at the end avoids walking from head to the last node.

sections.heading: Operation Complexity
sections.content: To estimate linked list operation cost, ask: **Do we already have the target node, or do we need to traverse first?**
items: **Head insert/delete**: update the head pointer directly, time complexity [[success|O(1)]].
**Tail insert**: [[success|O(1)]] with a tail pointer; [[warning|O(n)]] without one because you must traverse.
**Search / access by index**: move from head node by node; worst case [[warning|O(n)]].
**Middle insert/delete**: [[success|O(1)]] if you already have the previous node; if you only know an index, finding the position still costs [[warning|O(n)]].

sections.heading: When To Use It
sections.content: Linked lists are strong for **local rewiring, node reordering, and pointer techniques**.
items: Good for frequent insertions or deletions at the head.
Good for **fast and slow pointer** patterns: finding the middle, detecting cycles, or locating an intersection.
Good for reversing a list, merging sorted lists, and removing nodes by reconnecting links.
When data size changes often and random access is not needed, it can feel more natural than an array.

sections.heading: When To Avoid It
sections.content: Linked lists are usually not the first choice when the problem needs:
items: Frequent reads of the **k-th element**, because each access starts from the head.
Cache-friendly sequential scans; scattered nodes reduce locality.
Index-based binary search or fast jumps; arrays are usually better for that.
Small, simple data where pointer bookkeeping costs more than it helps.
